title: Sewn multi-section zine
subtitle: Binding a zine of several folded sections with a simple three-hole pamphlet stitch
excerpt: A zine with more pages than a single sheet allows benefits from proper binding. Three folded sections sewn together with a pamphlet stitch and wrapped in a card cover produces a booklet of up to 24 pages that opens flat and survives repeated use without the pages loosening.
sourceNotes: Pamphlet stitch binding for zine formats is standard in DIY bookbinding guides. The technique derives from traditional pamphlet and booklet binding; no single primary source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small group of pages folded as a single unit, also called a signature or quire. In a zine, each section is typically two to four sheets of paper nested together and folded to form a self-contained folded booklet. Multiple sections are sewn together at the spine.

A simple three-hole sewing pattern that runs a single thread through the spine of a folded section and ties off at the centre hole. For a multi-section zine, each section is sewn individually and then all sections are nestled together and bound as a whole.

content.text: zine is limited to the number of sheets that can be nested without the outer pages pulling away from the spine. Three to four sheets maximum is the practical limit. For a longer zine, sewing multiple sections together gives a flat-opening booklet that holds its shape. The
is the simplest functional sewing pattern for this purpose.

Fold two sheets of 90 gsm cartridge paper together to make each section. An A4 sheet folded gives an A5 four-page section; three sections give twelve content pages plus a cover. Crease each section's spine firmly with the bone folder. Stack the sections in order.

Cut the cover from bookbinding board at the same height as the folded sections plus 3 mm (1.5 mm top and bottom overhang) and wide enough to wrap around the spine. Score the cover spine with a bone folder pressed against a metal ruler: one score at each spine shoulder allows the cover to open without cracking.
Wrap the cover around all sections stacked together. Clamp or hold firmly. The spine should be as tight as the sections will allow.
Mark three sewing holes on the cover spine: one at the centre, one 1 cm from the head, one 1 cm from the tail. Pierce through all layers with the bookbinding awl.
Thread the needle with 30 cm of linen thread. Begin at the centre hole, pushing the needle from the outside in. Leave a tail of thread outside.

Pass the needle up through the inside of the booklet to the head hole. Push out, pull through. Come back through the centre hole from outside in. Pass down to the tail hole, push out. Return through the centre hole from outside.

The thread now exits the centre hole twice, passing along either side of the long stitch that runs the full length of the spine. Tie the two thread ends together over the long stitch at the centre. Trim, leaving a 5 mm tail.

A plain card cover can be decorated before assembly: linocut, stencil, watercolour wash, rubber stamp. Add a title on the front cover and table of contents on the first inside page to give the zine a professional structure. For a longer print run of 10 or more copies, a long-arm stapler through the spine is faster than hand sewing and produces an equally durable result.
